channel.title: Obsidian
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Surds & Quadratics
item.description:
This page was last modified on May 8, 2026 at 2:54 PM.This topic will investigate Surds and Quadratics.When numbers cannot be expressed as simple fractions, they are called irrational numbers and they cannot be written as a ratio between two integers. An example is pi()Irrational Numberscannot be written as a ratio between two integers.A common form of irrational numbers are Surds. NoteA surd is an irrational number that can be expressed by a root, typically a square root. They are represented using a radical().Now, not all things that are under a radical are surds: , are roots, but is not, as it is equal to 2. If you calculated the numeric value of a surd, you would find that the decimal points never end and never repeat. The number under the square root is called the Radicand.One of the key things to be able to do with surds, is being able to simplify them. Let's look at simplifying List the perfect squares. (we can stop there) Find the square that is lower than half of ; anything above this is not worth looking at as it can't multiply with a whole number to equal Find the largest square that will factorise into Factorise the surd Separate the surds Simplify That's it! In reality, it's around three steps, but the full process was shown to make it intuitive. Once you get the hang of it, it’ll be: Identify perfect square factors(if it's a square root; if it’s a cube root, find the perfect cube), Rewrite the [radicand]{The number under the radical} as the product of the perfect square and remaining factors. Simplify by taking the square out The essence to simplifying roots is just to find the largest square factor. In fractions, it is generally preferred to have a rational number in the denominator. is not acceptable, but is. For fractions involving surds, rationalising involves eliminating the surd from the denominator by multiplying the numerator and the denominator by a value. Anything done to the top must be done to the bottom to keep it equal: by multiplying the original fraction by a new fraction in the form of , the new fraction equals 1, so the numeric value of the term remains the same; only the form has changed. Typically, the value is the surd on the denominator.Rationalise the term To rationalise the in the denomiator, the numerator and the denominator is multiplied by i.e. . Surds can only be added or subtracted if they have the same radicand, similar to adding and subtracting like terms. these surds cannot be added. Just think of it as like terms in algebra. When multiplying or dividing surds, multiply/divide the radicands] together and simplify(if possible), provided they are the same ‘power’.Notice how when multiplying two surds, it can be condensed as one surd with the two radicands multiplied together, and when dividing two surds, it can be condensed as one surd with two radicands divided together.Note

This sub-topic will discuss the quadratic function. The specifics of functions and relations will be discussed shortly. There are two forms that quadratics can be written in: Polynomial Form, & Turning Point Form Polynomials are specific types of functions. They are made up of variables, coefficients and exponents that are whole numbers, combined using only addition, subtraction and multiplication. They follow the, intially, confusing form of: Let’s look at a typical example: The polynomial is of degree 2: the highest power of is 2. The 7 is a constant, so it does not involve , represented in the formula by . Here, 3 and 5 are coefficients, represented in the formula, by . This example is known as a quadratic, or a parabola. is also a polynomial. It is of degree 1, as is to the power of . We just don’t write that. It has a constant of two. So, what is a polynomial and what isn’t?
The general form of a quadratic is:Notice how the terms are written in descending order of power i.e. highest power first, then the second highest etc. This is also called the standard form.The turning point form of a quadratic is:To convert a polynomial to turning point form will require completing the square. Converting from turning point form to general form is just a matter of expanding the brackets. This form is also known as vertex form. The turning point form shows key features of the quadratic, as well as any transformations: The value of will either widen or narrow the curve. A negative will flip the curve down. will translate in the positive -axis direction. will translate in the positive -axis direction. is the turning point of the parabola.Try it out below: The Factorised Form of quadratics is: Note how in factorised form, the terms are all multiplied together, so they are factors. Compare this to the general form, where the terms are all added together. The discriminant allows one to determine the number of roots for an equation. When there are two functions, one being a quadratic and another being a function, and the number of intersects are needed, the discriminant can be used. The discriminant, if , then:
To factorise monic quadratics i.e. , you want to think of which two numbers multiply to give and add to give . Take What two numbers add to give and multiply to give ? We then split the middle term up into these values: Notice how the numeric value remains the same. We are only changing the form. Group by finding which terms have common factors(which ones you pick it doesn’t matter; there are two ways this can go and both are correct; pick which suits you & is faster): Take out the common factors of each: Notice how both terms have something in common? We can combine these two: Factorised! To show how it doesn’t matter how you group the terms when first finding the factors, I will do it the other way. It doesn’t matter because you are just deciding which terms to factorise first: they will produce the same answer. It doesn’t matter which order you multiply by, so the answer is still the same. Factorising also gives you the roots outright! To factorise non-monic quadratics takes one little extra step. Take . We need to find what numbers multiply to give , and adds to . The rest of the process is the same!

That’s all great, but what about if it cannot be factorised? Take . Nothing multiplies to and adds up to . This is where the Quadratic Formula comes in. If a quadratic cannot be factorised, or solved ‘by inspection'(i.e. by looking at it and thinking), the quadratic formula can be used. The quadratic formula will always work. It just takes a little while, so if other methods can work, use those.The Quadratic Formula!You will definitely, definitely need to memorise this. This will come up so many times over the next two years, and it's not on your formula sheet. x equals negative b plus minus b squared minus four a c over two a. <- This needs to be your personal "Methods Mantra".
Let’s take one of our previous examples: , which expands to . We can rearrange this to: Therefore, the roots are: & Graphing the function produces:
As we can see, the roots, where the functions value is , is at & . The answers for are the -intercepts of the graph. This is why the solutions to quadratics are called ‘roots’ or ‘zeros’. When we solve a quadratic, we find the values of , when is equal to zero. Hence, the answers for are the -intercepts.Completing the square is done when you have something in the format with no . But a is needed to factorise. First, divide all terms by , to achieve a monic quadratic. Then: Halve, square, balance Take
Here’s a summary of the translations possible.
And here’s an interactive diagram to help it make sense: where , , and are constants where: the vertex is at the axis of symmetry is at is a constant where: and are the roots is a constant

item.title: Probability
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:10 PM.The probability that an event occurs is given by the formula: where is the sample size. If then the event is impossible. If then the event is certain. The event , is the compliment of event , which means when does not happen. For example, if is the probability of rolling 1 and 2, on a 6 sided dice, then is the probability of not rolling 1 and 2, or the probability of rolling 3, 4, 5 and 6. Example Some people were asked whether they liked strawberry ice cream or chocolate ice cream. 82% said they liked strawberry ice cream & 70% said they liked chocolate ice cream, and 4% said they liked neither.
This is another way of representing the same data.
Tree diagrams are best when there are several events occurring. For example, a 3 sided die is rolled and then an item is picked from a hat that has 3 balls and 7 cubes.
However, if some things from the same set are being picked, there are two types, without replacement or with replacement. There are two ways it can go. A bag with red and blue balls. If I pick two balls, I could pick the first, and return it to the group, then I could pick the second. When I pick the second, the contents of the bag are the same as when I picked the first. However, I could also pick the first ball, and not replace it, and pick the second. Now, the bag's contents are different when I pick the second. If there are 6 blue balls and 3 red balls, the probability of me getting a blue ball is and the probability of me getting a red ball is . If I reach in and pick a ball and I get a red ball, and I don't replace it, the probability of me getting a blue ball is and the probability of me getting a red ball is . This is because there is one less red ball, also meaning there is one less ball in the bag. So, without replacement, taking an object reduces the sample size by one, and reduces the sample size of the object you took, by one.This is the probability of one thing occurring if another has already, i.e. what is the probability that A occurs given B has. The probability of A given B is written as: or
To calculate this, using P(A|B), cut down the sample space to what is given, which is B. Then, look for the event, which is A, in the sample space. Cut it down to B, and then look for A.For and to be independent: Note: Independent events just mean that one doesn't affect the probability of another occurring. One occurring doesn't make the other biased. At least one event occurring can be written as 1-P(none occurring) randbetween(min,max) vlookup(number to look for, where, which column, exact?) countif(set,instances of number to look for)

item.title: Combinatorics & Polynomials
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:10 PM.Combinatorics, at its heart, is the area of mathematics concerned with counting. It spans many fields of mathematics, due to the commonality of the need to count numbers. However, the field of combinatorics we will learn discusses combinations. It is used to great depth in probability, as it works with events with numerous outcomes. Factorials are represented by an exclamation mark . The factorial of a number is when you multiply that number with all of the whole numbers smaller than it, and is written as , said as "n prime". For example, Mathematically, is: One is not written as it is redundant.What are arrangements?Arrangements involve a set of objects, where the order of these objects is important.If we have a set: , a different arrangement might be CAB, or BCA. These are entirely different arrangements, because although the same objects[letters] are being used, the order of them are different. If you have 8 cars, and you want to park 5 on showcase, it’s written as . Of course, you got 8 cars because you were really good at maths! To find the number of ways to arrange objects, in groups of size , the following formula is used:AbstractTake the following example: We have 5 books, three of which will be displayed on a shelf. How many different ways can the books be arranged on the shelf?There are 5 books, and groups of 3: There are 60 possible combinations that the books can be arranged.What are combinations?Combinations involves the grouping of objects, where order is unimportant. Picking pens out of a pencil case, is an example of a combination. It doesn't matter what order the pens are in. Mathematically, it is when you pick r objects from a group of n objects, where order is unimportant. If you have 7, and you pick 3, that is written as 7C3. The only things that are counted are the objects. In the original set of , the combinations possible are: AB, AC, BC. AB & BA are considered the same as they use the same letters.The number of combinations of objects out of objects is:Let’s look at an example:There are 10 cards left in a deck, and 3 cards are drawn. What combinations of cards can be drawn? There are 120 combinations possible.Another form that combinations can be represented as, is the form of: Combinations are very important for the Binomial Theorem, which is used to expand binomials. Binomials consist of two terms, and are in the form of .Another thing that is very important, is the Pascal’s Triangle. We start at the top by writing 1, then we write two one’s in a triangle format. Then we write 1’s down the side of the triangle, and in each position in between, add up the two numbers above it.
Here’s a graphic to illustrate the process:
And here’s what a pascals triangle to .
This is pascal's triangle formed, by leading edges of 1's, and then adding two numbers next to each other to make the next number directly below:Pascal's triangle also represents combinations.
Pascal's Triangle has uses for us in combinatorics, and expanding.Expanding binomials can be done by hand relatively easy when its a quadratic, but when it becomes a cubic its a pain. When it gets to a quartic its a nightmare. There is a method to binomial expansion that makes it straightforward to expand any binomial to any power.
It is fairly simple in practice. Whatever the power, look to that row of the pascals triangle. All of the numbers in that row will be the coefficients of each of the terms. Then, the x term starts at the power and goes down each time, and the y term starts at power 0 and goes up each time.Let’s look at . Here, , so we look at that row of the pascals triangle. We read the numbers in that row: 1, 3, 3, 1. No we take our two terms and multiply them together, to get , and then we multiply them to each number, and add them together: Then, we take the first term, , and we make the first have the power of , and for each one afterwards, we make the power one less, until it reaches . We do a similar thing to the second term, , but the other way around. It starts at the power 0, and increases by one until it reaches the power of , at the final term. And you have completed the expansion! This process will work for binomials to the power of 7, the same process. It is so much faster than expanding at higher powers. Remember how combinations can also represent the pascals triangle? Well, I’ll show you how to calculate the terms without drawing up the triangle every time. Our power was 3. To find the first coefficient(we always start at 0). To find the first coefficient, we do the combination Finding the second, third and fourth coefficients: & & Thus the expansion would be: Hence, the Binomial Theorem is:InfoThis isn't a thing where you memorise the formula. It's rather clumsy and unhelpful. This is something where you understand the logic behind it. This needs practice to make it stick.The general term for a Binomial Expansion, to find any term, is: The general form for a polynomial is The degree, or order of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable. The leading term is the term containing the highest power. The constant term is the term containing no variable, it's just a number.
The powers must be natural numbers i.e. 1, 2, 3. There must be no division of the x terms. Therefore, the rule for polynomials, is that the powers must be non-negative. This is the sigma notation for a polynomial. The sigma notation represents a summation. The '' gets substituted into the equation on the left, and it starts at 0(the bit at the bottom) and goes up by one and ends at n(the bit at the top), and all of the instances of the equation on the right with each term is added up.
This might be read as, the sum of aixi, where i goes from 0 to n. The polynomial is a monic polynomial if the leading coefficient, the number outside the leading term, is just 1.
Every cubic must have one root, and this is due to their nature, in which they extend both ways in the y axis infinitely. There are two 'types' of cubics that will be studied. One with a stationary point of inflection, and one with turning points, and three roots. Every cubic always has at least one root. Every odd degree polynomial() will have one root somewhere, because they extend in both directions of the y axis.
Solving cubics, for our purposes, only involves factorisation, or by using a calculator. There is no handy quadratic formula, for cubics. Quadratics can be factorised into two terms(linear factors) e.g. , as two ’s multiplied give . Cubics can be factorised into three linear factors, or one linear factor and one quadratic factor. The plan of attack is to factorise into one quadratic and linear factor. Then determine if the quadratic can be factorised. The first step, surprisingly, is to guess. To work on solving large polynomials, we need to determine one factor. So, we substitute in numbers in and calculate until we strike one that is a root. In exams, questions of this type usually have a root between -3 and 3, close to 0. They are testing you on your ability to factorise, not to guess whacky numbers after all. Here’s a tip: guess factors of the constant, as the value of a root has to be a factor of the constant! Find the first factor of Guess first root: Think of factors of -21: (1,21), (3,7) Let's try 1: Let's try -1: Let's try 3: !! We've found one root! When , , thus the factor is: Then, to determine the quadratic, we need to divide the cubic by this factor. This is where it gets a little itchy. We need to do long division. Let’s keep working on our previous example: with a factor of We first set up our maths: the thing being divided(the dividend) goes inside the division, and the thing dividing(divisor), goes on the left. Our working out descends downwards, and the answer(the quotient) is written on the top.The first move is to look at the first term of the cubic, which is , and look at the first term of our divisor(), which is . What is divided by ? The answer is , so we write that above the .Then, we multiply our little piece of answer just worked out, , by the divisor, ), and subtract it from the cubic, by writing it underneath:And then we calculate that: I’ve removed the zero as it is not needed, but I just put it there for illustrative purposes: we have eliminated the . The term that is being worked on should be eliminated with each run of long division. Then, we pull down the rest of the terms of the dividend, that we haven’t worked with yet:Phase one completed! Now we repeat the process for the first term in the new dividend: Here, we will be working with the value we have just pulled down. We will not divide the cubic, but the quadratic under the line. Divide by , write the answer up top, and multiply this with the divisor & subtract:Phase two completed! Now we repeat the process for the first term in our new dividend: Divide by , write the answer up top, and multiply this with the divisor & subtract.We know we have completed the division as we have no remainders, and we have divided a cubic to get a quadratic. By dividing the polynomial by a factor, the remainder should be zero. Thus, you should always get zero. If you do long division, and you don’t get zero, this is a very good indicator that you have made a mistake somewhere. And it is very common to make mistakes doing long division. Long division is one of those things that you do not rush doing. We now know two factors of the original factor, one linear factor and one quadratic factor: In this example, the quadratic cannot be factorised, but if the quadratic can be factorised, that should be done to get the three solutions to the cubic. This quadratic cannot be factorised, due to the value of the discriminant; thus it holds no solutions; thus the only solution for this case is the linear factor. Graphing the quadratic indeed shows it has no roots:
Graphing the cubic indeed shows that it only has one root at :
A simple example is :
The general form for this is: where (h,k) are the coordinates of the stationary point of inflection. The stationary point of inflection is where the graph goes flat. What makes it characteristic and different from a turning point is that it keeps going in the same direction as it was before. In the image above, the point of inflection occurs at (0,0), and the graph was rising before this point, and it continues rising after this point.
The general form for this, is: where a, b, and c are the roots. To solve for the roots in this format, y must equal 0. The null factor law can be applied here to solve a cubic just like a quadratic.

item.title: Functions and Relations
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:10 PM.Number Sets Natural Numbers: Integers: Rational Numbers: Real Numbers: Positive Real Numbers: Negative Real Numbers: Set Notation A set: {...} Ordered Pairs: Infinite Ordered Pairs: Element of: Not an element of: Subset: Union: Intersection: Null/Empty Set: For all: There exists: There does not exist: Not: \ Interval Notation Including: [] (On graphs): Excluding: () (On graphs):
A relation is any set of points on a Cartesian plane. Anything on a Cartesian plane is a relation, including inequalities.A function is a relation with at most one -coordinate for each -coordinate.Functions can be written such thatIf all values of a variable are possible within the specified interval, the relation is continuous.If a relation only takes fixed values, it is discrete.The mapping is written formally as:
This isn't often used, it's just something that needs to be known.One x value to one y value. It is a function.
One x value to several y values. It is not a function
Several x values to one y value. It is a function
Several x values to several y values. It is not a function.
The domain is the set of all values for which the function is defined. We write .The range is the set off all y values for which the function is defined. We write .A piecewise function is just a function with different parts. It is a function with a few functions stuck together.It is continuous if there is a continuous flow, there is no stop. In simple terms, if you can draw it without picking up your pen, it is continuous. The last function has to end where the next takes off.It is discontinuous if one function does not join where the other left off, then it is discontinuous.Attention all personnel. Due to last night's groping, tonight's movie will be shown with the lights on.A Circle is the set of all points that are equidistant from a fixed point.Let the distance be and the fixed point be
The distance formula givesthereforeIdentify the centre, domain and range of centre = Radius = Domain = {x: }Radius = {y: }Deduce the two values of so that the point lies on the circle Complete the square twiceIf lies on the circle thentherefore
The square root function is the sideways parabola in f(x) form
The simplest reciprocal, or hyperbolic function is :Domain: is the vertical asymptote. is the horizontal asymptote.
as approaches 0 from the right, y approaches as approaches 0 from the left, approaches Correct notation for the above: Intercepts(if they exist) Asymptotes Vertical: Horizontal: Domain: {} Range: {} Calculate behaviour near the asymptotes Trigonometry is the maths about triangles, and triangles inside circles. And the weird stuff they can do.Also known as the trigonometric triangles, these two triangles give the sin, cosine and tangent values for 30, 45, and 60.

And here, we should introduce the #Unit_Circle, a circle with the origin at and a radius of 1.
What is are the coordinates for a point on the circle?
As the equation of the unit circle is (centre @ 0,0 & radius = 1)A worked example of finding the coordinates of a point:
The unit circle definition of is
If one had , to convert to radians, one would realise that is . The 360 fractions are equal to , so in radians is Radians start from the -axis:
The symbol for radians is .To convert between radians and degrees: Radians to degrees: Multiply by Degrees to radians: Multiply by Whatever you are trying to convert to, that's on top. Converting to degrees, 180's on top. Converting to radians, 's on top. Worked Example:

An arc is just part of the circumference of the circle.There are two arcs:
The arc length can be determined by multiplying the radius over the angle subtended by the arc. This can only work with an angle in Radians.Worked ExampleDetermine the arc length of a circle of radius 10cm subtended by an angle of 40.

So, as radians will be our default, how can we convert our magic triangles?
Well, there are 4 lots of 45 in 180. And is equal to 180. Therefore, 45 is equal to

There are three 60's in 180 degrees, therefore 60 is There are six 30's in 180 degrees, therefore 30 is



What happens if we try to graph sin? What if the variable sin is acting on is x?Like this:
Notice how it goes from 0 to in one wave, then to in anotherIn short, it does one full cycle in .The domain is The range is However, a big defining factor of sin is that it starts at 0 and goes up.Let's have a look at
It's very similar to , right?It is wavy.The domain is The range is But, it starts at 1 and goes down.It also does one full cyclone in .This is how the sine wave was created from.
If you took a wheel, and you rolled it, then you would get this:If you then make a mark on the wheel, then you tracked the height, you would get this:What about ?
Why is this? Because When These occur every units starting at Let's look at
Max: 1 {defined by unit circle} Min: 1 {defined by unit circle} Midline: y=0 Amplitude: 1 Period: Domain: Range: {} The midline is the line through the middle of the graph. The Amplitude is the distance from the midline to the minimum or the maximum. Basically, its the height of the peaks and the depth of the troughs. The period is the distance of which the function repeats.Determine exact values for
Determine exact values for
Let's try a harder one: Determine exact values for
Note: If the value that the trig function equals is not something that can be found in the exact value triangles, then it is a question that requires a calculator E.g. However: is not an exact value right???
The general form of the sine curve is: where: vertical dilation vertical translation (equilibrium position) horizontal dilation horizontal dilation / phase shift

item.title: Differential Calculus
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:10 PM.Average rates are simplifications. The average speed of an object is described by or Using the distance against time, the rate of change is speed. Graphing distance against time will yield a gradient of speed. The green line shows a constant gradient, which means the speed is the same. The blue line shows a positive constant speed, and then a stop, where the speed is 0(the gradient is 0, it's flat), and then a negative constant speed, returning to the start position.Generally, up and right are positive, while left and down are negative. 'Pick' two points Join them with a line Find the gradient. It gives an estimate of the gradient between those two points. This is where a point is taken and then another point is taken, and then an average value is calculated. Calculating an average speed needs two speeds. An example of this is an average speed camera spread over a highway, with two stations that calculate the average speed using the time taken and the known distance.This is where a single point is taken, and an instantaneous value is calculated. Calculating an instantaneous speed needs one speed. An example is a speed camera that gets the speed of a car as it passes.If you take two points on a function and get the average gradient, and you draw them together, you get closer and closer to the tangent at that point. By calculating the gradient as the difference between the two points() approaches 0(you want it to be as close to 0 but not 0 so you actually have a gradient), the following is produced: Here is a worked example.
The derivative of a function describes the instantaneous gradient of the function. The gradient function for the function x2 is 2x. This means, if I want to find the gradient of x2 at an x value, I plug that x value into the derivative, 2x. If I wanted to know the gradient of x2 at x=0.33, I simply plug 0.33 into 2x and I get the gradient being 0.66.The trick is to, in the f(x+h) bit, substitute x+h everywhere where it says x in the original formula. For example, There is a very straight forward way of calculating derivatives. Drop the power and take away one You take the power of and you multiply it("dropping the power"). Then you subtract one from the power("take away one") Here's an example.
There are three things to keep in mind when differentiating. The first is that any index under 1 needs to be written out of the fraction with a negative power. You must write it this way before using the power rule, otherwise it all gets mixed up and wrong. Here's what I mean:
The second, is that any radical can be arranged as such:The general rule is this:
The power of the thing inside goes on top. The power of the radical does underneath.The third is that any constant is ignored. This is because the constant represents the vertical shift, or in a straight line, the y intercept. This has no impact on the gradient, which is what the derivative represents. The derivative of any constant is 0. And as is also , whenever there is just , it goes away. Take 4. Drop the power, take away one. 4 x 1This is an example: The addition rule states that the differential of two bits added together is the same as the differential of each bit added together. An example is this: This is quite useful when there are large and ugly functions with many bits inside them; you can tackle each part one by one. means the derivative with respect to . is the thing that we are working with. We can change the bottom to match whatever we are working with. the derivative with respect to the derivative with respect to The tangent is a line 'touching' the curve at one point, and each point on a curve has its own tangent. The normal is a line that is perpendicular to the tangent.If the tangent has a gradient of , the normal has a gradient of .The equation of a line, with the gradient , through points & is:Note: A Secant is just a line between two points on a curve. AUCA2600009728
The way to know the gradient of a line, if you know the angle it makes with the -axis, is by using The position of an object is denoted by and is relative to an origin, usually . In physics, any object or body is referred to as a "particle". I don't know why. In general: If , the particle is located to the left of the origin If , the particle is located to the right of the origin If , the particle is located at the origin
Displacement, written as , is the position relative to the time, hence .If provided with a function for displacement(i.e. location), then the derivative of it gives the velocity. If , the particle is moving to the left If , the particle is moving to the right If , the particle is stationary The derivative of a function allows the nature of the original function, and its stationary points, to be determined. Stationary points are where the derivative is 0. In other words, there is a point where it is flat.
To determine the nature of a function with its derivative, draw a sign diagram. This uses the positivity or negativity of a derivative to inform the slopes of the function. Take the derivative .
Essentially, if you have a function and want to sketch it, find the derivative. Then find where it equals 0. These are your stationary points. Then find the slope to either side and draw a sign diagram, then sketch the curve from that. To draw the function, calculate the roots and intercepts, and then link up the curve sketch on the points.The rough plan to sketch a graph is this: Identify Intercepts Identify Asymptotes if relevant Calculate Stationary Points Classify The Stationary Points Determine the 'direction' or 'shape' of the graph as with a sign diagram Not in the formula book, but still very useful as it saves time.The chain rule is used where there is a function inside a function, and you basically get the derivative of one part and string it on to the next.If where u is a function of , then and: It's the derivative of the outside function multiplied by the derivative of the inside function.
This is the better product rule to remember. It's been rearranged to be easier to remember for the quotient rule.
The Quotient Rule is used when a function is divided by another function.

item.title: Methods Cheat Sheet
item.description: ^^ These are on your formula sheet. They are just a reminder. solving log and e stuff: solver is okay but, graphing each side is better, it gives a visual representation and number of solutionslook for quadratics; it's a lot easier to work with. if one side equals , you can multiply it by whatever you want to 'arrange' it into a quadratic. !

item.title: Indices & Exponentials
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM.Just a quick recap of index laws. It is possible to get quadratics inside an exponential expression. To solve these, one must substitute a value to reduce the expression to a quadratic, then solve the quadratic and then solve the value.If there is one solution, solve. If there are multiple solutions, graph it. The simplest form of an exponential is . The thing that defines an exponential is that the power is the variable. To solve for the exponent in equations of the form : Express both sides as powers of the same base Equate the indices and solve the equation formed to obtain the solution to the exponential equation.
Investigating :
So, as the base increases, the graph gets steeper. The asymptote is 'pointed' to the left. Notice how all graphs pass through , as , therefore, the base will not impact the -intercept.Investigating :
Notice how as the base is less than one, it flips around so the asymptote is pointed to the right. The smaller the base, the steeper it is.Investigating :
Notice how a negative power, leads to the graph also being flipped such that the asymptote is pointed to the right. As the base gets larger, the graph gets steeper.Clearly, negative powers and bases less than one lead to the graph pointing it's asymptote towards the right. A function with both negative powers and bases less than one lead to the graph pointing towards the right.
Note: there is a difference between: . The second is not a function, as it causes the data to repeatedly flip between positive and negative to an infinite scale.Having a negative out front inverts the function across the -axis:
Note how in all of the cases above, the horizontal asymptote is always at , regardless of the base. With a base greater than 1, the higher it is, the steeper the curve. With a base less than one, the lower it is, the steeper the curve.Below is an example of the effect of negatives:
The General Form is: where: vertical translation horizontal translation years( shifted right; shifted left) horizontal asymptote at -intercept at -intercept found by solving range The shape is defined by: Steepness: The bigger the base above positive/negative one, or the smaller the base between positive/negative one and zero, the steeper. Up/Down: Whether the exponential carries off into positive/negative infinity depends on the sign of the whole function. If the function is positive, it points up; if it is negative, it points down. Left/Right: Whether the exponential's “tail” points left or right, is dependent on the sign of the power. If the power is positive, it points right; if it is negative, it points left. RememberHorizontal shifts are the other way around. moves left by , while moves right by . Euler's Number (pronounced 'oiler', yes, 'oiler' you read that right), is , and is represented by What makes special, is that when , .
This can be calculated from first principles:
NoteNote how the was taken out from the limit. This is because it is independent from approaching zero, and is multiplied by what is dependent. This is known as a multiplicative constant. This can be explained by: Therefore, when What is a logarithm?Most operators in Mathematics have an inverse operation. Exponentiation is raising something to a power, so the inverse is a Logarithm. The logarithm of a number is the exponent which a base must be raised to, to produce that number.RememberFor the equation , one of the following must be true: or , because results in a mess. Additionally, because It's probably better to think of logarithms as an alternate way of writing exponentials, where the thing being 'equalled' is the power.You can only take logs of positive numbers!!Logarithms can be mathematically described as:
The base is little, and the result is the bit getting 'logged'.
ExamplesUnfortunately, you will need to know these... ;(For , the laws are:The Log LawsTo change the base of a logarithm, use the following formula: A logarithm with base is known as a natural logarithm, and is written as . A graph of has the following attributes: Vertical asymptote @ -intercept @ (1,0)
Just something worth recapping, as this caught me off guard: This is because the power rule does not apply. It only applies when the power is a number. In this case, the power is a function(), so the chain rule is used. It is not a case of , but in the power, there is something happening with , so the power consists of a function.Therefore, the following can be extrapolated: When differentiating an exponential, it is not straightforward to find the derivative. The way to solve these are to convert the base into a natural logarithm, then use differential calculus.
Rule for Differentiating ExponentialsTherefore, it can be concluded that: This is because can be written as , then To find the derivative of logarithmic functions, they need to be rearranged and differentiated with respect to . There are two rules to know for differentials involving logarithms. Let's start with
NoteTherefore: Combining this with the change-of-base law, can assist us to compute derivatives of logarithmic functions, with any base.
AbstractTo calculate the derivative of , use Now, let's look at . This is valuable, as you’re liable to come across the natural log of a function.
Thus: Here's a worked example where is cleverly broken up:
The inverse of a function is reflected across .
To calculate the inverse of a function: Swap & , Solve for , This is your inverse function, . Here's a worked example:
It's important to keep in mind that the inverse of a function does the exact opposite of the function. Therefore:If a differential is demarcated with a dot, it refers to the fact that it is differentiated with respect to time.Second derivatives represent how the ‘change’ occurs. If a function maps the displacement, the first derivative represents the speed, and the second derivative represents the acceleration. To calculate the second derivative, just calculate the derivative of the derivative; do the derivative maths twice. The second derivative will tell you the concavity; the way that the graph ‘points’ or ‘curves’.Consider the following graph:
The second derivative describes the following:
What the sign of the second derivative shows is which way is being pushed. Imagine a rolling car. A negative second derivative means the car is being pushed/accelerated backwards. It could be rolling forwards and being slowed down, and then backing up. A positive second derivative means the car is being pushed/accelerated forwards. It could be rolling backwards, slowing down, and then rolling forwards. This brings us to the second derivative test, which can quickly identify if a point of inflection is a local maximum, or a local minimum.When graphing a function, you will need to label the point of inflection, as well as the turning points, x and y intercepts etc.

item.title: Linear Motion
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM.A Force is a push or pull that exerts something on an object. Force has both Magnitude and Direction. It is a vector, just like Velocity and Acceleration & Displacement are also vectors. Scalars have only magnitude, and no direction. They include speed and distance.The difference between Distance and Displacement is that distance is just how far has been travelled. The displacement is the overall change in position. To convert from to , look at each part on its own. To convert km to m, multiply by 1000. To convert hours to seconds, multiply by 60x60, 3600. However, the unit is divided by hours and seconds, so the conversion is multiply by 1000 and divide by 3600. The conversion factor becomes, from , to
Negative displacements usually mean down, or backwards. Or south or west. To calculate the displacement linear-ly, add up the individual distances. For example, someone walks 14m east and then walks 2m west and then walks 3m east. where: final velocity initial velocity Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Often, the average velocity or acceleration is used, as during the span of a thing moving, sometimes it moves slightly differently. For example, a sprinter will take time to get up to speed and then reach top speed and then slow down from there. Therefore, often the average value is used instead. This is also the gradient over an area. To calculate the average over a certain span:
use the formula: There are 5 important motion formulas, used when acceleration is constant. Where: final velocity initial velocity acceleration time displacement An object continues to be at rest or move with a constant velocity unless acted on by a force. The acceleration of an object in direct proportion to a force applied to it and inversely proportional to its mass. where: mass(kg) acceleration() To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Pully and cart system A cart weighing 1.5kg is being pulled by a hanging mass that weighs 2.5kg. Calculate the acceleration of the cart.
The important thing to note here is that to calculate the acceleration of the cart, you use the force on the cart, divided by the mass of the whole system, as the whole system has to be moved, not just the cart.The steps are: Calculate the force exerted by the hanging mass () Calculate the acceleration from the force on the total mass Momentum was described by Albert Einstein as: "Momentum is mass in motion" And it is a great way to understand momentum. It is designated the letter and is calculated by: The SI unit for momentum is or The Law of Conservation of Momentum states that "the change in momentum for any system must be zero". Impulse is the change in momentum, represented by or , or It has the units . The momentum of a car is transferred to a wall during a crash. This generates an equal and opposite force on the car. The faster the collision occurs, the more force is exerted. The longer the collision occurs, the smaller the force is. Therefore, to survive a car crash, crumple zones slow down the collision time, increasing survival rates.Impulse is the area under the force-time graph.Work done can be defined as the product of force causing the displacement of an object, and has the unit of Joules. A force of 1 Newton applied over 1 metre does one joule of work.
#WIP Kinetic energy is the energy that a body posses due to motion. It is calculated by: where: mass(kg) velocity(m/s) This is energy stored by a body due to its position in a gravitational field, and is represented by: where: mass(kg) gravitational acceleration(9.8)(m/s/s) height(m) The law of conservation of energy states that 'Energy can neither be created nor destroyed'. Rather, it is converted between different forms. Often it is 'lost' because it bleeds away energy as it turns into heat or sound.The percentage of energy that is effectively transformed by a device is known as the 'efficiency' of the device.

item.title: Physics
item.description:
#CoreNode
Heat Processes
Electricity
Uncertainty & Error
Nuclear Physics
Linear Motion
Gravity & Electromagnetism
School
FolderContains 0 folders, 8 notes..spaceFolderContains 4 folders, 15 notes.AssessmentFolderContains 0 folders, 10 notes.DocumentsFolderContains 1 folders, 64 notes.DrawingsFolderContains 1 folders, 10 notes.HomeworkFolderContains 1 folders, 1 notes.TextbookFolderContains 1 folders, 3 notes.Unit 1Forces in 2D are described by vectors, which are values that hav...24/04/2026, 21:08:19This page was last modified on $= dv.current().file.mtime.toForm...24/04/2026, 21:08:20Note$$v =u+at$$24/04/2026, 21:08:20NoteWe’ve got plenty more practice questions for you to use to revis...24/04/2026, 21:08:20Accuracy is the ability to obtain the correct measurement. Syste...24/04/2026, 21:08:17This page was last modified on $= dv.current().file.mtime.toForm...24/04/2026, 21:08:20

item.title: Waves
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM.A wave is an oscillating, propagating form of energy. There are two forms, mechanical and electromagnetic waves. They transfer energy from one point to another. Electromagnetic waves do not require a medium to travel through; they can travel through a vacuum.Here is a simple graph of a wave:- Wavelength(): This is the distance from the start to the end of the wave. The wave repeats, so this is the distance for the wave to get to where it started; peak to peak, trough to trough. It can't be just to the same y value, it must have the same slope as well. Amplitude(): This is the distance from a peak or trough to the centerline. This determines how much energy the wave carries. Frequency(): This is the number of waves per second. Period(): This is how long it takes for a full cycle to occur. The relationship between frequency and wavelength is, as the frequency gets larger, the wavelength gets smaller where: is a constant, is the frequency(Hz) is the wavelength The phase velocity is given in terms of the wavelength and period as: The frequency is inversely related with the period such that: When wavefronts pass through a gap in a barrier, they spread, and this is called diffraction.
This occurs because every single point on a wave produces a little wavelet. Each molecule in a wave in water bumps water molecules in front of it, doing its part to transfer the energy. Waves can 'occur' at different times, if you drop one pebble into the water and then drop another quickly after it, but not at the same time, the waves will go up and down at different times. When this occurs, they are out of phase. When two waves are going up and down at the same time, they are in phase. Waves are often described as being out of phase by or , and the reasoning is below:
The way the wave propagates is called Huygen's Principle.
This is why waves 'bend' as they pass through a gap in a barrier.
Refraction occurs when a wave encounters a different medium, which has different characteristics. When it encounters a different medium at an angle(not head on) it bends and changes direction.
In this case air allows for faster waves than glass, and upon encountering the slower medium, the waves bend due to a speed change that occurs along each wave, causing it to turn; one side of the wave slows down first. Denser mediums allow waves to travel slower than less dense mediums, which allow waves to travel quicker. Depending on the difference in speed, the wave may bend more excessively, or less excessively to the normal of the medium.
An intuitive way to remember it is to imagine a little car driving along. When one tyre hits a slower or faster medium, which way will it turn?
The refractive index reflects how light travels through the medium as opposed to in a vacuum, and is therefore a ratio. It is calculated by:- where: refractive index speed of light speed of light in medium Snell's law describes the relationship between the angle of incidence and the refraction. The Angle of Incidence in the angle that the wave approaches the boundary between the mediums to the normal of the boundary.
Snell's Law states that: where: refractive index refractive index of medium 2 with regard to medium 1 phase velocity When two waves 180 out of phase(opposite) come across each other, they cancel each other out when they merge, forming a Node, but form again afterwards and continue. This is called Destructive Interference, as when the waves interfere, they 'destroy' each other.
A full cancellation only occurs if the amplitudes are equal.
If not, add the values(with one being negative), to find the total amplitude.When two waves in phase collide, they combine to have an amplitude that is the sum of both waves:
The peak is called an Antinode; it is the inverse of a node.This is the concept of superposition in waves, that when waves collide they 'add up', and the resultant wave is the sum of both. If one is negative compared to the other, they cancel; if both are positive they combine. Afterwards, they resume their original shape and direction.This can be seen in two pulse waves, of different sizes:

item.title: Nuclear Physics
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM.There are two particles inside the nucleus, that being the positively charged protons, and neutral neutrons. A nucleon is a particle in the nucleus.This is called the Nuclide Notation: The atomic number is called and the mass number is . The mass number is achieved by adding neutrons and protons. Therefore, the number of neutrons can be attained by: An isotope is an element that has a varied number of neutrons, be that more or less. As the mass of an element grows, it needs more and more neutrons to keep the atom stable. This is seen by the Segre Plot below:Why so many neutrons? Positive protons repel each other, so neutrons, which are neutral spread apart the protons so they don't repulse too strongly and blow apart the nucleus. Large nuclei have lots of protons so lots and lots of neutrons are needed to keep the atom stable. Neutrons are just there as stabilisers.When the atom becomes unstable and breaks apart, the emitted particles are known as radiation.Segre Plots show all stable elements in yellow with their isotopes:There are two forces inside the nucleus.This is a repulsive force that is formed by protons repelling each other due to like charges. This force is extremely strong.This is an attractive force that holds protons and neutrons together, and is the reason that atoms can stay together and exist. This force contains a lot of energy.In a nuclear fission bomb, this binding energy is released, and it is powerful enough to level cities. Yet it is only a tiny fraction of the SNF, less than 1%, and the core of the bomb only contains kilo's of uranium.This model describes the nucleus as a liquid, where the particles are constantly moving around, and the nucleus can 'wobble'. If it wobbles enough, it can split in two and release neutrons. A way of making it wobble enough to split is by hitting it with a high speed neutrons. Cherenkov radiation occurs when charged particles move through water at a speed that is faster than light can travel through water. It is like the light version of the sonic boom, and when this occurs, bluish light is emitted. This is possible as the speed of light in water is a lot slower than the speed of light in a vacuum. So what is being seen isn't actually the radiation itself, its the photons created by the sonic boom of the radiation. But if there's a blue glow, there's radiation... so step back!
Ionising radiation has the strength to knock out electrons from atoms, turning them into ions. This type of radiation can be very damaging. There are three types of ionising radiation to know.During nuclear decay the parent nucleus emits subatomic particles to produce a daughter nucleus, to gain stability. If the parent nucleus gains or loses a proton, a new element is created, and this is called transmutation. This occurs in Fission, where unstable nuclei break down.An alpha particle is a helium particle stripped of its electrons, travelling at a blistering speed. It is extremely large and is stopped by a piece of paper, however, it is the most damaging, as its large size and high speed give it a lot of momentum and ripping power. The symbol can be either or . Due to it missing all electrons, it as a charge.A beta particle is a high speed electron or positron. A positron is a positively charged electron, and basically the complete opposite of an electron. When a beta particle(positron) is released, a neutrino is released(written as ), which is a tiny, neutral and almost massless particle. One proton turns into one neutron When a beta particle(electron) is released, an antineutrino(written ) has almost the same properties as a neutrino, but is released with electrons. One neutron turns into one protonIf an electron and a positron collide, they annihilate each other and produce light. Beta Positive Decay Beta Negative Decay No change in nucleons occur. The unstable nucleus emits some energy as a gamma ray, making the nucleus "wobble" less. The parent nucleus with too much energy is written with an asterix to its right, like so: Identify the isotope of the daughter nucleus when plutonium-244 alpha decays. X=Uranium-240 Caesium-137 decays via beta negative decay X=Barium-137 Copper-64 decays via beta positive decay. X=Nickel-64Nuclear Decay can be modelled by the half-life. The half-life is the time taken for half of the radioactive nuclei to decay. The symbol is written like The equation is: where, is the number(or mass) of radioactive nuclei remaining is the initial number or mass of radioactive nuclei is the number of half-lives elapsed. The number of half-lives() is represented by: This formula needs to be memorised!A nucleus is fissile if it is just stable, so that when it is bombarded with a neutron, it becomes unstable.This is an example of a decay curve. Notice that it never approaches zero, the asymptote of the curve is at 0. It only gets closer and closer.
The gradient gets less steep over time as less radioactive nuclei are remaining and therefore less decay occurring. E = energy(J) m = mass(kg) c = speed of light (8x10)Subatomic particles released during nuclear decay typically have energies of only Joules. This is described as an electron volt, 1eV. An electron volt is the energy of an electron in an electric potential of 1 volt. Both of these need to be memorisedWhere: Electrical Energy Potential() Charge Potential Difference Protons and neutrons, being super tiny, need their own unit of mass. This is called the unified atomic mass unit(), or the atomic mass unit(amu). It is equal to kg. It is defined as of the mass of carbon-12, as carbon-12 has 6 protons and 6 neutrons, even numbers of both.Using , it can be deduced that 1 of matter is equivalent to 931.6MeV of energy. This needs to be memorised!Because the nucleons inside the nucleus are held in place, they have negative potential energy. This makes the mass of a nucleus less than the mass of its individual parts. Potential energy is the same as binding energy. It is how much energy is needed to break apart the nucleus. The difference between the mass of a nucleus and its nucleons is called the mass defect. It's basically how stuck together they are. The mass of one proton is 1.007276. The mass of one neutron is 1.008665.The energy of the mass defect is equal to the binding energy. This needs to be memorised Calculating mass defect. This indicates how strongly a nucleus is held together and therefore its stability: Fusion occurs when two smaller nuclei are combined into a larger nuclei, which produced energy. Fusion releases significantly more energy, roughly 5 times more efficient.

item.title: Electricity
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM.Electric flow is generated by a charge difference. Electricity only flows if the circuit is complete. Since the electron is negatively charged it flows from the negative side of the batter to the positive side. Resistance is measured in Ohm's (), and is referred to as The charge of an electron has Coulombs, therefore if one Coulomb of charge has passed through a point, then The current, the amount of charge passing a point per second, is given as: where: = Current(Amperes) = Charge (Coulombs) = time (seconds) 1 Amp = 1 Coulomb/second = electrons per second The electric force travels at the speed of light (). This means electrons themselves actually travel at the speed of light, so why doesn't electricity travel at the speed of light? However, as the individual electrons travel, they bounce off the atoms inside the wire. This means the electrons drift towards the high potential at . Because the electrons ricochet off the atoms in the metal, the metal imposes a resistance. It also absorbs this kinetic energy and heats up. Potential Difference is the same thing as voltage. Because electrons repel each other, and this gives them potential energy. The potential difference of a battery is the energy that the electrons hold. When a current flows, the electrons are not used up but the energy they carry is used up by powering the circuit. Picture a bucket of water being held up in the air. A pipe wired to the bottom of it lets the water out through a circuit, which does something. How much the water wants to fall/ the force pushing it, is analogous to the potential difference, the force at which the electrons wish to flow through the circuit.
The potential Difference (V) is the amount of electrical potential energy(W) given to each coulomb of charge(q). It is the electrical potential energy available per unit of charge, described as: In a circuit, the greater the voltage the greater the current flowing through it. The greater the resistance the smaller the currrent will flow. This is Ohm's law: Where: V = Potential difference(V) I = Current(A) R = Resistance Therefore, current increases with potential difference. Ohmic resistors produce a constant resistance, as they obey Ohm's Law. Non-Ohmic resistors(like light bulbs) heat up and increase the resistance as current increases. These do not obey ohms law, and hence they are called Non-Ohmic resistors.
The Current is defined as the amount of charge, that passes through a point in a conducting wire per second. It has the unit amperes or amps (), which are equivalent to coulombs per second. The equation is:Where: the number of electrons that flow through a point the charge of an electron seconds As the current is the number of electrons actually flowing through a point, and voltage/potential difference is the strength of the force behind these electrons, there is a good analogy for these. Consider a water pipe. A wide water pipe will allow lots of water to flow through, so it has a high current. A narrow water pipe will allow less water to flow through, so it has a low current. A pressure washer has a lot more pressure behind the water flow, so it has a high potential difference, whereas a simple water hose has a lower potential difference. Current is the 'flow', and voltage is the 'pressure'.
An #Ammeter is a device that measures the current, and needs to be placed in series. It needs to be placed in the circuit to have the electricity to flow through it.
A #Voltmeter is a device that measures the potential difference, and can be placed in parallel to the circuit. It can just be placed alongside the circuit.
A #Multimeter can measure both the current as well as the potential difference. Typically they can do more, being able to measure the resistance, AC vs DC and lots of other things. Series means components are connected back to back, in a chain. Hence "series". Parallel means components are connected side by side, parallel to each other. These use junctions, in which electrons have several paths they can take.
The total resistances should add up such that the voltage equals to zero as it arrives back at the battery. The total resistance is equal to the resistances added up, if they are connected in series. Once the total resistance has been calculated, the current can be calculated using Ohm's law. As the current flows through both resistors, the current is the same for both resistors. However, as the voltage must be 0 as it reaches the battery, it decreases through each resistor. The voltage drop can be calculated for each resistor using Ohm's law.
To find out how much current flows through a resistor, out of several, you must: Find out how much resistance is present in total Find out the fraction of resistance the resistor poses compared to the total If resistors are in parallel, it means the current could flow through two paths.
To calculate the total resistance, this formula is used: Then, to calculate the current coming from the battery, use Ohm's Law. However, as the voltage must be 0 as it reaches the battery. Each path and therefore resistor must bring it down to 0 and therefore, the voltage is the same for both. Either way the electricity flows, it has to be reduced to 0 volts. Now, the current can be calculated either by breaking up the resistances and the current, or by using Ohm's Law.
The sum of potential differences around a closed circuit must be zero This means that the resistors use up all of the potential difference of the battery. The total current flowing into a junction equals the current flowing out

item.title: Heat Processes
item.description:
This page was last modified on April 24, 2026 at 9:08 PM. Energy is measured in Joules () Power is measured in Watts () #SI_PrefixesIn James Joule's experiment, he discovered that temperature is directly related to the motion of particles in a liquid.
#Temperature is the measure of the kinetic energy of the particles in a body of matter, which reveals itself as a vibration in the molecules. The faster and harder the vibration, the hotter the substance is. Einstein called kinetic energy "Energy in motion." However, temperature is actually the average kinetic energy(KE) of particles in a body, as the particles in said body form a population, and therefore there is a distribution of kinetic energies. Temperature is defined as

#Heat is energy in the process of being transferred from one place to another due to temperature difference.Why does hot air rise? Hot air contains molecules that have more kinetic energy; hence they can overcome gravity more than cooler air. The kinetic energy causes the particles to collide more and spread out, leading to lower density. Celsius uses 0 as the freezing point of water and 100 as the boiling point of water. Kelvin uses 0 as absolute zero, which is -273.15oC Kelvin made a thermodynamic temperature scale which determines the amount of kinetic energy in any given material. However, absolute zero, does not mean that there is no motion within the particles of a substance. It marks the minimum motion of a substance's particles. This is because of the Heisenburg's Uncertainty Principle, which states that for any particle, it is impossible to know its momentum and it's exact position at the same time. If it was theoretically possible to chill a piece of lead to where the motion of its particles is zero, then both the particles position and momentum would be zero, but measuring this would be forbidden by Quantum Physics; it would be impossible. Below 30oK, some substances become superconductive, meaning there is no electrical resistance. Hydrogen loses all surface tension and begins to crawl everywhere. Space is heated by microwaves from the Big Bang, leaving it at 2.73oK. The coldest natural place is the Boomerang Nebula, spitting out gas which cools it to 1oK. The coldest place in the universe is in laboratories on earth.Conversion Kelvin temperature = Celsius temperature +
#Conduction is the transfer of kinetic energy through collisions that occur due to contact between neighbouring atoms and molecules. It occurs between objects in direct contact.
#Convection is a transfer of heat caused by movement within a fluid which causes hotter and less dense liquid to rise and cooler and denser liquid to sink down and this forms a loop.
#Radiation is the thermal energy that is emitted in the form of electromagnetic rays(typically infrared) or photons.
#Specific_Heat_Capacity is the amount of energy that must be transferred to a material to change the temperature of 1 Kg of said material by 1oC. --> Amount of energy () --> Mass of substance () --> Specific Heat Capacity ()
#Latent_Heat_Energy must be added to break the bonds between water molecules for ice to become liquid. Molecules have negative potential energy so they require energy to break the bonds.
, where - The heat energy transferred/required (J) - mass (kg) - Latent Heat (J/Kg)
There are several different forms of latent heat. There is the Latent Heat of Vaporisation, written as and it is used for boiling and condensing. There is also the Latent Heat of Fusion, written as and it is used for freezing and melting.When two bodies of different temperatures meet, they equalise temperatures such that: Qlost=Qgained A calorimeter is just a device that measures heat.

item.title: Uncertainty & Error
item.description:
Accuracy is the ability to obtain the correct measurement. Systematic errors cause low accuracy. If one is measuring the heat of the reaction but reading the thermometer from an angle, they would always get a slightly elevated temperature compared to the truth. Precision is the ability to consistently obtain a measurement. Random errors reduce the precision. If one ran an experiment over the course of a week in a room, and the temperature varied, then the results would not be consistent even if everything else was done perfectly.To determine the percent error that an experiment is off compared to the true results, this formula is used. Uncertainty is a range of measurement from the experiment, within the true value is expected to lie. Errors are the differences between the true and the experimental values. This is not necessarily a problem with the experiment. True values are often impossible to attain. Error can never be eliminated, only minimised.Instruments are very powerful in the conclusions that are drawn. If one has a scale that says 10g, it is very different to a scale that reads 10.000g. The level of accuracy that the scale reads is key to conclusions drawn. The 10g scale could be weighing a mass at 9.8g or at 10.4g but will still read 10g. the error would be 0.5g. The scale that reads 10.000 could have a mass of 9.995g or 10.004g, and still read 10.000g, but in this case the variance is For a ruler, the gradient is every millimetre, so the error of the ruler is . So the uncertainty is always half of the graduation that it goes by. Absolute Uncertainty just represents the spread of the data from the average.
#Significant_Figures have to do with the scale analogy. It represents the accuracy of the data. The precision that data is recorded determines the number of decimal places due to the scale of the instruments used.
Note, for 1400, the reason it has 2 significant figures is because off the ambiguity in it. There is no decimal point to clarify, so 1400 could be rounded from 1350 and 1449, if the instrument only gave two significant figures worth and has a scale of 100. This is possible. The way to clarify if it is measured to a degree is by adding a decimal point() or in scientific notation( ).Significant figures are the number of digits in a sample not including leading zeros but including trailing zeros with a decimal point. The trailing zeros are important and must only be what was read off the instrument. If a thermometer read C then C would be misleading as well as C. Rules: All digits including 0 in the coefficient of a number written in scientific format are significant All non-zero digits are always significant(excluding calculation scenarios)(2.17 has 3 significant figures) All zeros between two non-zero digits are significant(3015 has 4 significant figures) A zero to the right of a decimal point and following a non-zero digit is significant(0.5700 has 4 significant figures) Any other zero is not significant as it will be used only for locating decimal places(i.e. 4200 and 0.005) If three volumes were calculated at , , and , it would be misleading to have an average of , as there were only 4 significant figures worth of precision. The extra 7 is considered a "non-significant figure". When making calculations, use the lowest number of significant figures present, as this is the most representative of the data. The least precise measurement limits the precision. For example, a highly accurate accelerometer with 6 significant figures paired with a timer with an output of 2 significant figures, the timer reduces the overall precision to 2 significant figures.When measuring with a device: For analogue devices, the uncertainty is equal to the smallest graduation. For digital devices, it is the smallest decimal point that it measures. For measuring cylinders, measure from the bottom of the meniscus The P-Value calculations works with the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. The null hypothesis states that whatever is under investigation has no effect. The P-Value determines how likely the null hypothesis is true, how likely the investigated variable does not actually influence the dependent variable. It involves two groups, one is the control and one is the test group. It cannot be used to compare averages or single values. It considers the size of the difference in the results, the sample size, and the variations in the data. It is a value between 0 and 1, and can be written as a percentage. A P-Value of 0.05 means there is a 5% chance that the null hypothesis is true in the investigation. A P-Value less than 0.05 is considered statistically significant.A fundamental law of statistics is that as the size of the sample increases, the sample average will get closer to the true population average. For the normal distribution to occur, the standard minimum number of sample points is .
